last_excerpt: can be adjacent and never really have occasion to think about each other. Someone told me once that county borders in the midwest are so straight because they were surveyed before the land was settled, so there was no natural feature to follow, no river to trace. The border just is where someone decided it was. And then people organized their whole sense of place around that line, generation after generation, until the line felt inevitable. I don't have a strong feeling about that, I just find it worth sitting with. There's a gas station near where I grew up that sits exactly on the county line. Half of the parking lot is technically one county and half is another. I'm not sure anyone who has ever stopped there for gas has thought about this. I'm not sure it matters. But it's there, that line, running right under the pavement.
